input: Can I trigger multiple deployment jobs from a single Jenkins job?
expected_output: Yes, you can trigger multiple deployment jobs from a single Jenkins job using the `build` step provided by the Pipeline Build Step plugin. Each downstream job can be called sequentially with its own parameters, allowing you to deploy multiple applications from one pipeline.
context: Can you make a job that triggers other jobs? Absolutely. the "build" action in pipeline will do it. Can you make a job that does multiple things? Sure, don't see why not, super easy to do in pipeline, and less easy to do in freestyle.

input: Why does the kubectl container hang or exit immediately in a Jenkins Kubernetes plugin pipeline?
expected_output: The kubectl container exits immediately when using 'cat' if the image lacks a standard cat binary, and hangs when using 'sleep' as the command. The Kubernetes plugin requires 'cat' with tty: true to pass commands into the container. The fix is to use 'command: cat' with 'tty: true' and add 'runAsUser: 1000' under the container's securityContext.
context:
[quote="lknite, post:3, topic:3316"]
e ‘cat’ command exited immediately, causing the ‘kubectl’ container to exit immediately
[/quote]

So I'm thinking that means it either doesn't have cat, or it's a non standard version. Without being at a computer I can't check.

I know the default command is cat to allow it to pass commands in and out of the container. I've never really looked how or why it works.

I wouldn't call it unstable, i would call it picky. I think it needs cat, or something like it, to translate java code into the container. Not sure about the specifics though. Its how the docker stuff works, not really k8s specific

can you please publish your files and pipeline with the correct configurations?

Was have the same issue. The fix for me was adding [[CODE_SNIPPET_5]].  

[[CODE_BLOCK_4]]

input: Why is Jenkins on Kubernetes getting BitBucket connection timeout errors?
expected_output: The Bitbucket connection timeout is likely caused by a firewall misconfiguration. Routing traffic without the firewall resolves the issue. Even if bitbucket.org is allowed, the firewall may still be dropping or throttling traffic inconsistently.
context:
- ![image|690x218](upload://64sTv4xkccznY4EeP3DB5CkDaS6.jpeg)

Hi @balakrishnanarchitec and welcome to this community :wave: 

Is there a proxy, a firewall, or any other network appliance between your machine and bitbucket?

Thank you for the update.

We are using the GKE cluster and yes, we have firewall in place. But firewall team confirmed that the bitbucket.org is allowed.

Sometimes it's working fine but some time it takes lot's of time to load.

the issue with firewall. As part of testing we have routed the traffic without firewall the connection with bitbucket is working as expected.

Thanks a lot for the feedback.

input: Is it possible to specify a kubernetes secret as the source for the private SSH key in JCASC plugin?
expected_output: Use the kubernetes secret provider - https://plugins.jenkins.io/kubernetes-credentials-provider/. This is recommended than defining said secrets in jcasc.
context:
Is it possible to specify a kubernetes secret as the source for the private SSH key in JCASC plugin?

I have this:

[[CODE_BLOCK_0]]

Where ssh-private-key is:


[[CODE_BLOCK_1]]
But nothing is created in the jenkins master credentials section.

Also looked at:

https://github.com/jenkinsci/configuration-as-code-plugin/tree/master/demos/credentials

but that does not have an example of using a k8s secret.

Any suggestions?

but that does not have an example of using a k8s secret.

Any suggestions?

if you use the kubernetes secret provider - https://plugins.jenkins.io/kubernetes-credentials-provider/ then you don't need to define said secrets again in jcasc.

Works like a charm, thanks!

input: How to disable auto update of Jenkins and Plugins on Jenkins restart when running on Kubernetes?
expected_output: Set `installPlugins: false` in the Jenkins Helm chart values to prevent the plugins from reinstalling plugins on restart. If a PVC is mounted for Jenkins Home, plugins won't need to be reinstalled every time. Alternatively, bake plugins into a custom controller image using the plugin-installation-manager-tool during Docker build, and use renovate to manage plugin version updates.
context:
installPlugins: false

Yes I was looking at that but hoped I could fix this problem without having to rebuild the whole controller image each time a new plugin needs to be added  - compared to storing them on a pvc and managing from values file.

But will give it a try, thanks! 

Would you then still keep the explicit versions in the plugins.txt file that is being read during the docker build?

I'm just telling you how to disable the helm chart installing things. if you have a PVC mounted for jenkins home, you don't need to reinstall each time.

[quote="dev-samples, post:3, topic:1662"]
Would you then still keep the explicit versions in the plugins.txt
[/quote]

https://github.com/jenkinsci/plugin-installation-manager-tool will have all the details, but you don't need to specify versions, you can just specify plugins and it'll grab the latest version each build.

input: Why is the GitLab API Token not showing in the credentials dropdown in Jenkins?
expected_output: The GitLab API token is not shown in the Git Plugin becuase it does not accept GitLab API credential types. It only accepts username/password credentials for HTTP/HTTPS for SSH access. To fix this, create a username/password type credential and set the username to token_name and the password to the token_value.
context:
![image|690x289](upload://f3rN5B8lMxSOR9Fnk1V2q6GIWNL.png)

It is most likely is that the credential type that you created is not the credential type that is expected in that context.  

The git plugin accepts username / password credentials for HTTP and HTTPS repository access and it accepts SSH private key credentials for SSH repository access.  It does not accept any other credential types.  It does not accept a GitLab API token credential.

Thank you for clarifying the types of credentials supported by the git plugin!

Ah! This looks like the same problem with the repositories of my students. The user experience is very poor in this case, I think we should rethink the UI here. 

The user selects "create credentials", the user creates a new "token", the dialog closes, but nothing to see, even no error message that the newly created token is not valid.

Hello,

How did you resolve it?

expected_output: The pipeline fails because `sh` is not available on Windows. Replace `sh 'cat $FILE'` with `bat "type $FILE" to access the file on Windows nodes.
context:

Replacing sh 'cat $FILE' with bat "type $FILE" works for Windows nodes...

17:55:57 at java.lang.ProcessImpl.start(Unknown Source)

”CreateProcess”… are you running that on Windows? Perhaps you don’t have a [[CODE_SNIPPET_1]] shell installed. Try [[CODE_SNIPPET_2]] instead.

input: Why do my Jenkins Jobs fail when I close my laptop? Not shutdown.
expected_output: Closing the laptop likely puts it to sleep, disconnecting it as a Jenkins agent. The Maven job type is not safe for agent disconnection and will fail when the agent goes offline. Switching to pipeline jobs is recommended as they are able to survive an agent disconnection.
context: Does your laptop maybe go into sleep after closing it? Is your laptop an agent of your Jenkins? Anyway you're using a the maven job type that is know to be problematic and contrary to pipeline jobs is not safe for disconnection of the agent. pipeline jobs are able to survive an agent disconnection.

input: How to change USERNAME and USERPROFILE in Jenkins system properties on Windows?
expected_output: USERNAME and USERPROFILE in Jenkins system properties are determined by the user account running Jenkins as a service. To change them, update the login information in the Windows service definition. It is recommended to use a dedicated service account instead of LocalSystem.
context:
These are under Manage Jenkins > System Information

Thanks!
D

I assume you are running Jenkins as a service on your setup? If so, to change the user that Jenkins is running as, you would need to change the login information in the service definition. USERPROFILE would use whatever the USERPROFILE value is for the user you specify as the login for the service. I would recommend using a service account for login, not LocalSystem.

Thanks Alex - that was it. I appreciate the assistance!

Migrating to a new version of Jenkins. All is going well so far, but I have run into an issue with the USERNAME and USERPROFILE.

This setup is on Jenkins 2.414.1. And my USERNAME for System Properties is my Machine Name and the USERPROFILE is some random windows file. I would like to change these, but cannot find a spot to edit these in the GUI or a config file to edit.

These are under Manage Jenkins > System Information

Thanks!
D

input: In Jenkins, does project, job and item mean the same thing?
expected_output: Yes, in Jenkins, Project, Job, and Item all refer to the same concept. In the user interface a project has the same meaning as a job. 'Job' is the older term while 'Project' is the preferred current term per the Jenkins glossary. 'New Item' was renamed from 'New Job' because it now also covers creating folders and other things, not just jobs.
context:
Project = Job =  Item.

In the user interface a project has the same meaning as a job. 

*New item* is not a good choice as an action name: it originally was named *New Job* (or *New Project* :wink:) but since you can also create new folders (or other things) it has been renamed to *New Item*. Which is technically correct, but the user interface should not focus on technical implementations...

We have a ["Jenkins glossary"](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/glossary/#job) that also provides some guidance on terms and usage.  In this case, it notes that "Job" and "Project" are referring to the same thing, with "Job" being the older term and "Project" the preferred current term.

It may say that but I don’t remember seeing project in use anywhere =/

In Jenkins, 
whether the [[CODE_SNIPPET_0]] equals to [[CODE_SNIPPET_1]]?

and when create a Job, there is the Tab:  New Item.

so the Item refers to Job right? 

Project = Job =  Item.

In the user interface a project has the same meaning as a job.

input: What backup methods are available for Jenkins?
expected_output: There are three main backup methods for Jenkins: filesystem snapshots, backup plugins, and shell scripts. Filesystem snapshots provide maximum consistency and speed, supported by LVM, btrfs, ZFS, and many cloud providers. Several backup plugins are available via Manage Jenkins > Plugins, though only the thinBackup plugin is currently maintained. You can also write a custom shell script to copy the required files to a backup location and schedule it using cron.
context:
reducing the possibility of copying different data at different time points.
They are supported by:
The Linux Logical Volume Manager (LVM)
Linux btrfs
Solaris ZFS (which also supports incremental backups)
FreeBSD ZFS
OpenZFS on Linux
Some other file system architectures
Many cloud providers
Some separate storage devices also let you create snapshots at the storage level.
Plugins for backup
Several plugins are available for backup.
From the main menu select
Manage Jenkins
, then go to

Several plugins are available for backup.
From the main menu select
Manage Jenkins
, then go to
Plugins>Available
and search for
backup
.
Note that only the
thinBackup Plugin
of the open source plugins is currently being maintained.
You can try the other plugins but you may have problems with them.
Writing a shell script for backups
You can write your own shell script that copies the appropriate files and directories to a backup location.
Use
cron
to schedule when the backup script runs.

Use
cron
to schedule when the backup script runs.
The shell script should create a directory such as
/mnt/backup
to which the backup will be written;
be sure that you have write permissions to that directory.
Consider creating
/mnt/backup
as a separate filesystem with its own mount point.
An alternative method is to create a subdirectory in
/var
.
Note that if you use this method,
you might need to use the
sudo
command to execute the restore operation.
Backing up to
/tmp
is not advised because

input: How does Blue Ocean handle SSH key generation when connecting to a remote Git repository?
expected_output: Blue Ocean automatically generates an SSH public/private key pair or provides an existing pair for the current Jenkins user. This credential is registered in Jenkins with the domain 'blueocean-private-key-domain', ID 'jenkins-generated-ssh-key'. You must then configure the SSH public key on the remote Git server by adding it to the authorized_keys file of the Git server user's .ssh directory before proceeding.
context:
If your URL is for a remote Git repository, be sure your URL starts with either:
ssh://
- which displays as
ssh://gituser@git-server-url/git-server-repos-group/my-git-repo.git
or
user@host:path/to/git/repo.git
- which displays as
gituser@git-server-url:git-server-repos-group/my-git-repo.git
Blue Ocean automatically generates an SSH public/private key pair or provides you with an existing pair for the current Jenkins user.

This credential is automatically registered in Jenkins with the following details for this Jenkins user:
Domain
:
blueocean-private-key-domain
ID
:
jenkins-generated-ssh-key
Name
:
<jenkins-username> (jenkins-generated-ssh-key)
You must ensure that this SSH public/private key pair is registered with your Git server before continuing.
If you have not already done this, follow these two steps.

If you have not already done this, follow these two steps.
Configure the SSH public key component of this key pair (which you can copy and paste from the Blue Ocean interface) for the remote Git server’s user  account (e.g., within the
authorized_keys
file of the machine’s
gituser/.ssh
directory).
This process allows your Jenkins user to access the repositories that your Git server’s user account has access to. Refer to the
"Setting Up the Server"
of the
Pro Git documentation
.

input: What are the benefits of using Docker as agents in a Jenkins Pipeline?
expected_output: Using Docker containers as agents allow the pipeline to define the environment and tools they required without having to configure various system tools and dependencies on agents manually. This approach allows you to use practically any tool which can be packaged in a Docker container, making pipelines more portable and flexible.
context:
Docker
images and containers to
run inside. This allows the Pipeline to define the environment
and tools required without having to configure various system tools
and dependencies on agents manually. This approach allows you to use
practically any tool which can be
packaged in a Docker container
.
For more agent specification options, consult the
syntax reference
.
Jenkinsfile (Declarative Pipeline)
[[CODE_BLOCK_0]]
Toggle Scripted Pipeline
(Advanced)

input: Which plugins help filter potentially unsafe environment variables in Jenkins?
expected_output: Three plugins help filter unsafe environment variables in Jenkins: - Safe Batch Environment Filter automatically filters Batch build steps if environment variables contain Batch metacharacters. - Generic Build Step Environment Filters provides generic filter implementation for other script interpreters. -Pipeline: Keep environment Step filters out unused environment variables in pipelines to prevent a Global Configuration from failing build steps.
context:
Safe Batch Environment Filter
allows administrators to automatically fail Batch build steps (both in classic projects and in Pipelines) if those steps would have environment variables with Batch metacharacters.
Generic Build Step Environment Filters
provides generic implementations for build step environment filters that can be used to filter environment variables for other script interpreters.
Pipeline: Keep Environment Step

Pipeline: Nodes and Processes
2.36 and newer.
This functionality can be used to change or remove variables containing potentially unsafe metacharacters (such as
^
or
&
in the case of Windows Batch) from the environment of these build steps, or even fail the build step.
The following plugins implement functionality that is useful for filtering out potentially unsafe environment variables:
Safe Batch Environment Filter

input: How are Jenkins plugins typically built and packaged?
expected_output: Most Jenkins plugins are built with Maven, using the Jenkins plugin POM as their parent POM which provides a default build configuration. The Maven HPI plugin handles the heavy lifting such as bundling plugins in the HPI/JPI archive format used for Jenkins plugins, and allows developers to run a debug Jenkins controller with the plugin.
context:
Plugin Build Process
Most Jenkins plugins are built with
Maven
.
They typically use the
Jenkins plugin POM
as their parent
POM
, which provides a sensible default configuration for the plugin build.
The
Maven HPI plugin
is one of the plugins configured there.
It does the heavy lifting, such as bundling plugins in the HPI/JPI archive format used for Jenkins plugins, or allowing developers to run a debug Jenkins controller with the plugin.

input: How can you combine retry and timeout steps in a Jenkins Pipeline?
expected_output: The retry and timeout wrapper steps can be composed together in a Jenkins Pipeline. For example, if we wanted to retry our deployment 5 times, but never spend more than 3 minutes in total before failing the stage, you can nest retry inside timeout or vice versa. Wrapper steps such as timeout and retry may contain other steps, including each other.

flakey-deploy.sh
script 3 times, and then
waits for up to 3 minutes for the
health-check.sh
script to execute. If the
health check script does not complete in 3 minutes, the Pipeline will be marked
as having failed in the "Deploy" stage.
"Wrapper" steps such as
timeout
and
retry
may contain other steps,
including
timeout
or
retry
.

input: Why should Jenkins builds be isolated from each other?
expected_output: Builds need to be isolated from each other because build scripts running on the same agents may be able to access data related to other unrelated projects and potentially manipulate their build results. Even without malicious intent, badly set up build scripts can accidentally delete unrelated data or cause resource exhaustion on the static agent, delaying other builds.
context:
An additional concern is that builds need to be isolated from each other:
While simple Jenkins instances building a single project may be able to operate securely with a single static agent, administrators of more complex instances need to consider whether builds need to be isolated from each other.
Some examples:
Jenkins hosts multiple teams, which are not allowed to access each others' projects or source code.

Jenkins hosts multiple teams, which are not allowed to access each others' projects or source code.
Jenkins builds pull requests/merge requests submitted to public repositories, e.g., on GitHub.
In these scenarios, build scripts running on the same agents may be able to access data related to other, unrelated, projects, and potentially even manipulate their build results.

input: What are the two ways to write CLI commands in a Jenkins plugin?
expected_output: There are two ways to write CLI commands in a Jenkins plugin. The first is annotating a method with @CLIMethod, which is the easiest approach when exposing behaviors of model objects as CLI commands. The second is extending CLICommand as a subtype and annotating it with @Extension, which provides lower-level control and is suitable for commands that require more serious terminal interaction and remote code execution.
context:
Writing commands can be done in two ways.
Annotating a method with CLIMethod
If you are exposing behaviors of your model objects as CLI commands, the easiest way to achieve it is to put
@CLIMethod
on a method of your model object. See
Queue.clear()
as an example.
In addition to the command name as specified in the annotation, you also need to define "CLI.command-name.shortDescription" as a message resource, which captures one line human-readable explanation of the command (see

input: What are the prerequisites to start using the Adobe Cloud Manager Jenkins plugin?
expected_output: Before using any builders or steps in the Adobe Cloud Manager plugin, Jenkins needs to be configured to authenticate to an Adobe IO project. This involves three steps: creating an Adobe IO API Integration, adding the credentials to Jenkins, and configuring an Adobe IO Project.
context:
https://www.jenkins.io/jep/229
Getting Started
Before using any of the builders or steps in this plugin, Jenkins needs to be configured to authenticate to an Adobe IO project. To do that, follow these steps:
Create an Adobe IO API Integration
.
Add the credentials to Jenkins
Configure an Adobe IO Project
AdobeIO WebHook
This plugin supports receiving AdobeIO Cloud Manager events. See the
Adobe IO Project configuration
for details on the WebHook operation, and the
Adobe IO documentation

input: What can you manage using the CA Service Virtualization Jenkins plugin?
expected_output: The CA Service Virtualization Jenkins plugin lets you manage virtual services and provision tests from the Jenkins UI. Key features include creating, deploying, starting, stopping, and undeploying virtual services, deploying tests and test suites, viewing built-in test reports, using global or dynamic CA SV connection settings, storing credentials in Jenkins Credentials store, leveraging Jenkins parameters to parametrize CA SV build steps, and full support for Jenkins Pipeline scripting.
context:
CA Service Virtualization Jenkins Plugin
The CA Service Virtualization plug-in for Jenkins lets you manage virtual services and provision tests on CA Service Virtualization from your Jenkins UI.
Key Features:
Create and Deploy virtual services
Start / Stop virtual services
Deploy / Undeploy virtual services
Deploy tests and test suites
Display of test or test suite Jenkins built-in reports
Use global or dynamic CA SV connection settings

Use global or dynamic CA SV connection settings
CA Service Virtualization credentials supported by Jenkins Credentials store
Leverage Jenkins parameters to parametrize CA SV build steps
Full support of Jenkins Pipeline scripting
Follow the
CA Service Virtualization Jenkins plugin community
to get the latest news, tips & tricks, best practices and support.
Additional Information
For detailed documentation see:
Jenkins Plug-in for CA Service Virtualization.

input: What are the four testing modes available in the API Fortress Jenkins plugin?
expected_output: The API Fortress Jenkins plugin supports four testing modes. 'Run single test' runs a single test by providing a test ID. 'Run automatch' runs tests by providing a URL representing a certain endpoint using automatch patterns. 'Run by tag' runs multiple tests marked with a certain tag. 'Run project' runs all tests contained in a project.
context:
Fortress account.
Modes
There are 4 testing modes available. "Run single test" will run a single
test, while the others will run suites of tests.
Run single test
You can run a single test by providing a test ID. Test IDs can be found
in test interstitial pages.
Run automatch
You can run our "automatch" mode by providing a URL representing a
certain endpoint. You can configure automatch patterns in the
"Automatch" section of each test. Read more about automatch
Here
.
Run by tag

"Automatch" section of each test. Read more about automatch
Here
.
Run by tag
By running "by tag" you can run multiple tests, marked with a certain
tag. Tags can be added and edited in the test details.
Run project
By running a full project, you'll be running all tests contained in the
project.
Options
The following options can apply to any mode.
Blocking
The plugin can silently run (blocking = false), let the build continue
with a success and inform you if the test failed using the various

input: How do you setup and use the calendar view plugin?
expected_output: To set up the Calendar View plugin, first create a new view by selecting the 'Calendar View' option and giving it a name. Then configure the view by selecting the jobs whose builds should be displayed and customizing settings as needed. The View Job Filters Plugin can be used for more flexible job selection. Once opened, the view displays all past and future builds for the selected jobs.
context:
About
Shows past and future builds in a calendar view.
Screenshots
Features
Provides a month, week and day view of past and future builds
Indicates status of past builds by color
Displays when future scheduled builds will happen
Shows estimated duration of future scheduled builds
Configurable date and time settings
Usage
1. Create a new view
Select the
Calendar View
option and give the view a name.
2. Configure the view

Select the
Calendar View
option and give the view a name.
2. Configure the view
Select the jobs whose builds should be displayed in the view and customize the view to your liking:
Tip: to be more flexible in selecting the jobs use the
View Job Filters Plugin
.
3. Open the view
This will show all the past and future builds for the jobs that you have selected in the previous step.
Note: there is currently no auto refresh available.
Past builds
Past builds will appear in four different colors:

input: Is there a way to categorize Jenkins jobs by naming patterns in a view?
expected_output: Yes, the Categorized View plugin allows you to create a 'Categorized Jobs View' that groups jobs using regular expressions. In the configuration page under 'Job group categorization', you can add multiple expressions, each with a regex to match job names and a naming rule for the category label.
context:
Introduce a new kind of view where you can categorize groups of jobs under a single label created by regular expressions or other criterias that can be implemented as extension points.
Description
This plugin allows you to create a new view that is very similar to the standard Jenkins List Views, but where you can group jobs and categorize them according to regular expressions. The functionality is better explained with images.

input: How can I find out if my Jenkins instance has security vulnerabilities or plugin issues without manually checking everything?
expected_output: The Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees plugin proactively identifies issues in your Jenkins environment including security vulnerabilities, performance problems, and plugin version issues. It scans a Support Bundle generated daily from your Jenkins controller and emails you a summary whenever a new problem is detected. The first report covers everything found, and follow-up reports only highlight changes.
context:
Introduction
Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees (formerly known as "CloudBees Jenkins Advisor") proactively notifies you of problems with your Jenkins-based environment. Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees identifies numerous issues before they affect your users, including security vulnerabilities, performance problems, and plugin version issues. Best of all, Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees is constantly being improved by the
CloudBees Support

Initially, Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees sends an initial report that lists everything it detects in your system. Subsequent reports will only report changes from the initial report. It’s important to note that Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees only emails you if it finds something wrong: if it doesn’t find any problem in your system, it won’t send you email.
Jenkins Health Advisor by CloudBees identifies problems by scanning a Support Bundle generate by the
support-core

input: How to configure build log compression plugin?
expected_output: Open job configuration and check the "Compress Build Log" and save. From then on, when builds finish their buildlogs will be compressed to a log.gz file, after which the original log file would be removed. Jenkins will transparently access the log.gz file when the build log is viewed or downloaded.
context:
About
The Compress Build Log Plugin allows to configure jobs to have their
build logs compressed. For a long time, Jenkins has supported showing
gzipped build logs on the UI and through its APIs, but had no facility
to compress build logs. This plugin implements that.
Configuring build log compression
In a job configuration, check "Compress Build Log" and save. You're
done! From then on, when builds finish, their build log will be
compressed to a
log.gz
file, after which the original
log

compressed to a
log.gz
file, after which the original
log
file will
be removed. Jenkins will transparently access the
log.gz
file when the
build log is viewed or downloaded.
Pipeline Compatibility
From version 1.1, the Compress Build Log Plugin supports compressing
full pipeline build logs.
Note:
Pipeline: Job
plugin
prior to version 2.26 stored per-step build logs. This plugin does not
support compressing these, only the main log file. As of version 2.26

input: How do I send Jenkins build notifications to a Discord channel?
expected_output: The Discord Notifier plugin bridges Jenkins and Discord using Discord's built-in webhook functionality. After installing, go to your job's configure section, add the Discord Notifier item, and enter your webhook URL. You can configure options like sending only on state change, sending only on failure, linking artifacts, and adding thumbnails.
context:
Discord Notifier provides a bridge between Jenkins and Discord through the built-in webhook functionality.
The purpose
The Jenkins Discord Webhook plugin was made to share results of a build to a Discord channel using the webhooks that Discord provides.
Through this plugin you are able to:
Get success and fail messages about your build
Link to build artifacts
List SCM changes to the build
Only send notifications on state change
Download
Discord notifier is available in official jenkins repos.

Download
Discord notifier is available in official jenkins repos.
Usage
This plugin uses the post-build feature to execute a request.
After installing, go to your job's configure section and add the
Discord Notifier
item. Then proceed to enter your webhook URL.
There are a few options you can choose from:
Webhook URL
The URL of the webhook (pretty self-explanatory) provided by Discord
Send only on state change

input: How can I track which version of my application is deployed to which environment?
expected_output: The Environment Dashboard plugin creates a custom Jenkins view that displays a matrix of deployed code versions across environments. Rows represent your applications and columns represent environments like CI Test, Performance Test, Pre-production, and Production, with each cell showing the deployed version. To set it up, configure your deployment jobs to publish deployment records to the plugin, then create a new view from the Jenkins home page.
context:
Jenkins Environment Dashboard Plugin
This Jenkins plugin creates a custom view which can be used as a dashboard to display what code release versions have been deployed to what test and production environments (or devices).
Click on the Environment name to get a historical view of the deployments.
Example set up
You have 3 software components:
UI
Web Application and API
Database

Pre-production
Production
You have Jenkins jobs for deploying build packages to your environments.
You find it hard to track which version of each application is currently deployed to.
You configure your deployment jobs to publish a record of each deployment to this plugin.
You create a view which displays a matrix with rows for your 3 applications, columns for your environments and the intersections with the deployed code version.
Other information:

input: How can I use GitLab accounts to authenticate and authorize users in Jenkins?
expected_output: The GitLab OAuth plugin secures a Jenkins instance by offloading authentication and authorization to GitLab using a GitLab OAuth Application. GitLab users are surfaced as Jenkins users and GitLab organizations and teams are surfaced as Jenkins groups for authorization. It supports multiple authorization strategies.
context:
The GitLab OAuth plugin provides a means of securing a Jenkins instance by offloading authentication and authorization to GitLab. The plugin authenticates by using a
GitLab OAuth Application
. It can use multiple authorization strategies for authorizing users. GitLab users are surfaced as Jenkins users for authorization. GitLab organizations and teams are surfaced as Jenkins groups for authorization.
More comprehensive documentation is listed on the
user documentation page
.
Useful links

input: How to setup Go in a Jenkins pipeline?
expected_output: The Golang plugin automatically installs and configures Go tools on a Jenkins agent during a build. First, go to Manage Jenkins > Global Tool Configuration, find the 'Go' section and add a Go installation with a name and version. For Declarative Pipeline, use the tools directive. For Scripted Pipeline, use the tool step and manually set GOROOT and PATH.
context:
Automatically installs and sets up the
Go programming language
(golang) tools on a Jenkins agent during a build.
Functionality
During a build, this plugin can:
Install a particular version of Go on the agent that the build is running on
The correct package for the machine's operating system and CPU architecture will be automatically downloaded and installed, if not already present
Export the
GOROOT
environment variable, pointing to the installed Go tools
Add the path
$GOROOT/bin

Export the
GOROOT
environment variable, pointing to the installed Go tools
Add the path
$GOROOT/bin
as a prefix of the
PATH
, so that the tools are available during the build
Usage
Once this plugin is installed, you must first configure which Go version(s) you need for your Jenkins jobs, and then configure any jobs that need Go as appropriate.
Global configuration

input: How do I install the Google Kubernetes Engine plugin in Jenkins?
expected_output: To install the Google Kubernetes Engine plugin, go to Manage Jenkins > Manage Plugins. Optionally click 'Check now' to refresh plugin data. Under the Available tab, search for 'Google Kubernetes Engine Plugin', check the box under the Install column and click 'Install without restart'. If the plugin does not appear under Available, check that it is listed under Installed and is enabled.
context:
The Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Plugin allows you to deploy build artifacts to Kubernetes clusters running in GKE with Jenkins.
Documentation
Please see the
Google Kubernetes Engine Plugin
docs for complete documentation.
Installation
Go to
Manage Jenkins
then
Manage Plugins
.
(Optional) Make sure the plugin manager has updated data by clicking the
Check now
button.
In the Plugin Manager, click the
Available
tab and look for the "Google Kubernetes Engine Plugin".
Check the box under the

Available
tab and look for the "Google Kubernetes Engine Plugin".
Check the box under the
Install
column and click the
Install without restart
button.
If the plugin does not appear under
Available
, make sure it appears under
Installed
and is enabled.
Plugin Source Build Installation
See
Plugin Source Build Installation
to build and install from source.
Usage
See the
Usage
documentation for how to create a
Deploy to GKE
build step.
Feature requests and bug reports

input: Why does a GitLab merge request webhook trigger a Jenkins build when a user is just assigned to the MR?
expected_output: GitLab sends the same webhook payload for every merge request event, including when a user is only assigned. To avoid unnecessary rebuilds, add a conditional stage to the pipeline that inspects the webhook payload and only continues when actual code changes are detected, since GitLab's documentation indicates certain fields are only present when new code is pushed or a suggestion is applied.
context:
Problem is, if there's an Open Merge Request, and after a while someone assigns a user to that Merge Request, that will trigger the webhook and it will build the Jenkins job again, even though it's not necessary.

Is it possible to not have the Jenkins job triggered when a user is assigned to the GitLab Merge Request? (I couldn't find anything online and in Jenkins, it looks exactly the same in terms of the build and environment variables)

Thanks ahead!

Hi :wave: ,

Could you write a conditional stage to the pipeline that deals with the webhook?
The [documentation](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/integrations/webhook_events.html#merge-request-events) says:

> The field [[CODE_SNIPPET_0]] is only available when there are actual code changes, like:
> * New code is pushed.
> * A [suggestion](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/reviews/suggestions.html) is applied.

input: How can I make a Jenkins job act as a link or shortcut to another job?
expected_output: There is no first-class shortcut job type in Jenkins. One approach is to install the markdown-formatter plugin and the embeddable-build-status plugin, then place a build status badge link to the target job in the description of the referencing job or folder.
context:
You might install the [markdown formatter plugin](https://plugins.jenkins.io/markdown-formatter/) in your Jenkins controller and the [embeddable build status plugin](https://plugins.jenkins.io/embeddable-build-status/) to place a link to the other job in the description of the referencing job or folder.

I have a folder like this that uses embeddable build status links in the page to show the status of key jobs in a much larger folder of jobs

input: Why does my Jenkins SSH credential fail to connect to a Linux machine?
expected_output: The SSH credential in Jenkins must contain the private key you generated, not the public key. The public key belongs in the target machine's authorized_keys file (unattended access via .ssh/id_rsa), and the matching private key must be stored in the Jenkins SSH credential used by the job.
context:
![imagen|509x300](upload://tzqbnPCGSnFbthxhifVjFfcsufN.png)

Hi Marc
Already on the verge of "despair" I have gone back to review the thread... (I am very surprised how in such a large forum about Jenkins, no one can shed light on something, I understand, everyday in Jenkins, like connecting to a linux machine or windows, but well that's another topic...) and what you said ***"You can look up how to set up unattended access: .ssh/id_rsa as your research point"***.. I think it's the solution.

Also the link that @mwp565733 mentioned fits very well to your problem it is exactly the same error message that you see. So you should check your credential in Jenkins that it contains the private key you created and not the public key.

input: Why does the Jenkins controller randomly disconnect from an agent on Linux?
expected_output: Automatic apt upgrades on the agent can upgrade the JDK during a build, which breaks the running Remoting connection between the controller and agent. Disabling automatic updates in 20auto-upgrades (or at least disabling automatic Java updates) stops the agent from being silently restarted underneath Jenkins.
context: EDIT: I did have automatic updates/upgrades switched on in 20auto-upgrades, when apt update/upgrade was used there was a jdk under the "the following packages will be upgraded...", when I checked back the main jenkins node I saw that it lost the connection to the agent. Chances are very high that the upgrades were the reason for connection drops. I will test this out more after updating packages and disabling automatic upgrades and will let you know if the connection keeps dropping again.

[[CODE_BLOCK_0]]
If you guys need any more info I'll be glad to provide. 

Main Node Jenkins setup:

[[CODE_BLOCK_1]]

Could you check the  java version on the agent before and after the job fails? Sometimes auto updates on java can cause the connection to fail. This can be solved by disabling auto-update on the java.

input: Why does an `sh` step hang inside a custom Docker image used as a Jenkins agent?
expected_output: If the Dockerfile never declares a USER, the container runs as root, and Jenkins cannot interact with the shell. Adding `USER jenkins` (or another non-root user that matches the workspace permissions) as the last instruction in the Dockerfile lets the `sh` step run normally.
context:
> The [[[CODE_SNIPPET_9]] instruction](https://docs.docker.com/reference/dockerfile/) in a Dockerfile is a fundamental tool that determines which user will execute commands both during the image build process and when running the container.

Since you never declared that the container should run as the [[CODE_SNIPPET_10]], it runs as the [[CODE_SNIPPET_11]].

Thank you so much. I was stuck on it for 2 days.

Thanks

[quote="psquared-dev, post:1, topic:35704"]
[[CODE_SNIPPET_7]]
[/quote]

I think that you need [[CODE_SNIPPET_8]] as the last statement in the Dockerfile.  A [Docker blog post](https://www.docker.com/blog/understanding-the-docker-user-instruction/) says:

input: Why does POSTing to `/user/<id>/descriptorByName/jenkins.security.ApiTokenProperty/revoke` return HTTP 400 "The tokenUuid cannot be empty"?
expected_output: The revoke endpoint requires a non-empty `tokenUuid` form parameter identifying the token to revoke. Sending an empty `tokenUuid=` value (or omitting it) produces a 400 response with the body "The tokenUuid cannot be empty", so the caller must look up the UUID of the token and include it in the form body.
context:
org.jclouds.http.internal.JavaUrlHttpCommandExecutorService - Receiving response -1829602346: HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
    jclouds.headers - << HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
    jclouds.headers - << Server: Jetty(9.4.39.v20210325)
    jclouds.headers - << X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
    jclouds.headers - << Cache-Control: must-revalidate,no-cache,no-store
    jclouds.headers - << Content-Type: application/json
    jclouds.headers - << Content-Length: 161
    jclouds.wire - << "{[\n]"

<< "Error 400 The tokenUuid cannot be empty[ ]" << "[ ]" << "

HTTP ERROR 400 The tokenUuid cannot be empty[ ]" << "URI:/user/admin/descriptorByName/jenkins.security.ApiTokenProperty/revoke[ ]" << "STATUS:400[ ]" << "MESSAGE:The tokenUuid cannot be empty[ ]" << "SERVLET:Stapler[ ]"

input: Why does the Jenkins LDAP plugin fail with "No subject alternative DNS name matching ... found" when talking to an internal AD?
expected_output: The LDAP plugin is trying to validate the TLS certificate presented by the domain controller against the hostname it was given (for example DomainDnsZones.mydomain.loc), and the cert from the internal CA does not list that hostname as a SAN. The fix is to either reissue the AD certificate so its Subject Alternative Names include the actual hostname Jenkins connects to, or to import the internal CA into the JVM truststore so the chain is trusted.
context:
I tryied but I obtained:

Oops!
A problem occurred while processing the request

Caused: org.springframework.ldap.PartialResultException: nested exception is javax.naming.PartialResultException [Root exception is javax.naming.CommunicationException: DomainDnsZones.mydomain.loc:636 [Root exception is javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: No subject alternative DNS name matching DomainDnsZones.mydomain.loc found.]]

I am using an internal CA 

Could you give me a suggestion?
Thanks a lot
Mario

input: What happens to the workspace when the same Jenkins job runs concurrently on one node?
expected_output: Jenkins automatically gives each concurrent run a separate workspace on the same node by appending a suffix to the workspace name. The same rule applies to the ws step. To avoid concurrent runs entirely, pipeline jobs have a configuration option to disallow concurrent builds.
context:
​

[quote="poddingue, post:5, topic:15073"]
As far as I know, when two builds of the same job are running concurrently, they try to use the same workspace, which can lead to one build being canceled or the builds interfering with each other.
[/quote]

No!
Jenkins will take care that you get separate workspaces, at least when they run on the same node. The second run should happen in a workspace with [[CODE_SNIPPET_2]] appended.

This also applies to using the [[CODE_SNIPPET_3]] step

Thanks a lot for correcting, @mawinter69 . 🤗

btw, pipeline jobs have a configuration option that disallows concurrent builds and that aborts previous builds.

![image|690x79](upload://9iygKtkOZ2EokXC48OGNJa6Smdf.png)

Thank you for help!
A temporary fix is to [u]disable[/u] concurrent builds, but the issue turned out to be more Kubernetes related than I thought initially.

input: Why does my global variable documentation not show in the Jenkins UI for a Shared Library?
expected_output: The Global Variable Reference for a shared library variable's .txt documentation is only available at the branch level of a multibranch pipeline job, not at the job level.
context:
So I have created my variable [[CODE_SNIPPET_0]] and the documentation at [[CODE_SNIPPET_1]]. Put them into [[CODE_SNIPPET_2]] folder of my shared library. Ran the associated job successfully, however the documentation in the Jenkins UI is not present.

Does this still supposed to work, or not anymore?

Never mind. Was looking at the job level, but the documentation is available only at branch level in multibranch pipeline job.

https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/shared-libraries/#directory-structure mentions that global variables can be documented in .txt files representing those variables, and this documentation appears in the **Global Variable Reference** window.

input: Why does an ephemeral Docker Android agent fail with `UnsupportedClassVersionError: class file version 55.0`?
expected_output: The agent image is running a different Java version than the Jenkins controller. Bytecode version 52.0 means JDK 8 and 55.0 means JDK 11, and the agent and controller must use the same Java version. JDK 8 is also no longer supported since Jenkins 2.357 and 2.361.1 LTS, so the agent image needs to be rebuilt with a matching modern JDK.
context:
Cheers
Gianpaolo

Hello and welcome to this community @gianpaolof :wave: 

You have to use the same version of Java on the agent and on the controller.
Could you please check if that's the case?
Bytecode version 52.0 is JDK8, and 55.0 is JDK11. 
Please note that JDK8 is not supported anymore since Jenkins 2.357 and 2.361.1 LTS.
If you need more recent Android Docker images, I have created quite a lot of them there: https://hub.docker.com/repository/docker/gounthar/jenkinsci-docker-android-base

Starting agent process: cd “/home/jenkins” && java -jar remoting.jar -workDir /home/jenkins -jar-cache /home/jenkins/remoting/jarCache 
Error: A JNI error has occurred, please check your installation and try again
 
Exception in thread “main” java.lang.UnsupportedClassVersionError: hudson/remoting/Launcher has been compiled by a more recent version of the Java Runtime (class file version 55.0), this version of the Java Runtime only recognizes class file versions up to 52.0

input: How do I run Jenkins behind Caddy as an HTTPS reverse proxy using Docker Compose?
expected_output: To run Jenkins behind Caddy, create a docker-compose.yml and a Caddyfile in the same directory. Prerequisites are DNS pointing to the Docker host and ports 80/443 open. After startup, set the Jenkins URL under Manage Jenkins > System > Jenkins Location to your subdomain, or to a sub-path like https://example.com/jenkins/ with JENKINS_OPTS=--prefix=/jenkins if using a sub-path setup.
context:
Index
Reverse proxy - Caddy
This sets up Jenkins behind Caddy as an HTTPS reverse proxy using Docker Compose.
Prerequisites
DNS for your domain pointing to the Docker host
Ports 80/443 open to the internet
(Optional, for sub-path) Decide your Jenkins context path (e.g.,
/jenkins
)
Files
Create these two files in the same directory.
docker-compose.yml
[[CODE_BLOCK_0]]
Caddyfile (subdomain)
[[CODE_BLOCK_1]]
Caddyfile (sub-path
/jenkins
)
[[CODE_BLOCK_2]]
Run
[[CODE_BLOCK_3]]
Jenkins URL

input: Which Jenkins directories do I need to back up vs. ones I can leave out?
expected_output: The exploded war file, ./cache (downloaded tools), and ./tools (extracted tools) do not need to be backed up as they can be re-downloaded or re-extracted. The ./plugins/xxx subdirectories will be automatically populated on the next restart. Always validate your backups so you do not discover they are unusable when you actually need them.
context:
— Exploded
war
file
To restore a system, download the latest
war
file.
./cache
— Downloaded tools
To restore a system, download the current version of the tools.
./tools
— Extracted tools
To restore a system, extract the tools again.
./plugins/xxx
— Subdirectories of installed plugins
These will be automatically populated on the next restart.
Validating a backup
Your backup strategy should include validation of each backup.
You do not want to learn that your backup is no good when you need it!

input: Why does Jenkins keep showing a reverse-proxy-broken error even though pages mostly load?
expected_output: Jenkins runs a self-check that depends on the reverse proxy correctly rewriting redirects and forwarding the original protocol. The most likely cause is that response rewriting is misconfigured or `X-Forwarded-Proto` is missing. Rather than rewriting the context path at the proxy, run Jenkins under the same path the proxy expects, for example `java -jar jenkins.war --prefix=/foobar`.
context:
so this will always get through provided the request is properly rewritten),
which will then redirect the user to another page in Jenkins (this only works
correctly if you configured the response rewriting correctly), which then returns 200.
This error message indicates that this test is failing - and the most
likely cause is that the response rewriting is misconfigured.
See the
configuration examples
for additional tips about
configuring a reverse proxy.
Be sure to set the
X-Forwarded-Proto

Much easier to change Jenkins to run at the context path your reverse proxy is
expecting, e.g. if your reverse proxy is forwarding requests at
https://manchu.example.org/foobar/ to Jenkins then you could just use
java -jar jenkins.war --prefix=/foobar
to start jenkins using
/foobar
as the context path

input: What is the major change to be aware of when upgrading to Jenkins LTS 2.504.1?
expected_output: As of 2.504.1, Yahoo! UI (YUI) has been removed from Jenkins core (initially removed in weekly 2.493). Plugins that still depend on YUI in their configuration UIs will break. A small number of plugins with over 800 installs (AWS Lambda, SLOCCount, OpenStack Cloud, Fortify on Demand) have not yet removed YUI, and others are tracked in the YUI removal epic.
context:
As of Jenkins 2.504.1, Yahoo! UI (YUI) has been removed from Jenkins core.
This was initially removed in the weekly 2.493 release and is the result of work done over multiple years.
Plugins that had previously used YUI must remove its usage accordingly.
Thanks to all of the work from core and plugin maintainers and contributors, there has been great progress made to ensure that components have been updated.
The list of plugins can be found in our
tracking sheet
.

The list of plugins can be found in our
tracking sheet
.
At this time, there are only a few plugins with over 800 installs, not planned for deprecation, that have not removed YUI from their configuration.
These plugins are:
AWS Lambda
SLOCCount
OpenStack Cloud
Fortify on Demand
There are other plugins, all under 700 installs, that have issues created for them in the Jenkins issue tracker, linked to the overall
YUI removal epic
.
Remove jCIFS and j-Interop

input: Which Configuration as Code and agent launch changes must I make when upgrading to Jenkins LTS 2.440.x?
expected_output: Replace `diskSpaceMonitor` with `diskSpace` in the JCasC nodeMonitors definition. For agents, the `-jnlpUrl ${JENKINS_URL}/computer/${AGENT_NAME}/jenkins-agent.jnlp` argument to agent.jar is deprecated; use `-url ${JENKINS_URL}` and `-name ${AGENT_NAME}` instead, passing in `-webSocket`, `-tunnel`, and work directory options as needed.
context:
The official Docker images are already using the new calling convention.
Replace diskSpaceMonitor with diskSpace in configuration as code
Improvements in node monitoring (agents and the controller) include a change in the configuration as code definition for nodeMonitors.
Jenkins LTS 2.426.3 and earlier used diskSpaceMonitor to configure the free space threshold for agents.
Jenkins LTS 2.440.1 and later use diskSpace to configure the free space threshold for agents.

Upgrading to Jenkins 2.440.1
Upgrade to the latest version of Remoting
The
-jnlpUrl ${JENKINS_URL}/computer/${AGENT_NAME}/jenkins-agent.jnlp
argument to the agent JAR has been deprecated; use
-url ${JENKINS_URL}
and
-name ${AGENT_NAME}
instead, potentially also passing in
-webSocket
,
-tunnel
, and/or work directory options as needed.

input: How does plugins.jenkins.io decide what is 'Trending' and how plugins are categorized?
expected_output: Trending is approximated by dividing the previous month's install count by the month before that and showing plugins with the highest ratio in descending order. Categories come from `label-definitions.properties` in the `update-center2` repository, and labels are GitHub topics assigned to a plugin's repository by its maintainers.
context:
Currently (2019-10) this just divides the previous month’s install count
by the month before that.
Plugins with the greatest numbers are shown in
descending order.
What are categories based on?
Categories are based on
label-definitions.properties
in the
update-center2
GitHub repository.
See
categorize the plugin
for further documentation.
How are plugins labeled?
Plugin labels are assigned to plugin repositories by their maintainers as
GitHub topics
.

input: How does a plugin tell Jenkins that a class implements an extension point?
expected_output: Mark the implementation class with the `@Extension` annotation. Jenkins discovers and registers `@Extension`-annotated classes that implement any defined extension point (an interface or abstract class) automatically, so they show up wherever the extension point is consumed.
context:
To register an implementation, make sure you mark it with
@Extension
.
The extensions index
is a useful list of the extension points used in Jenkins and Jenkins plugins.
More information can be found in the
Extensibility section
.

Extensions
This section is a work in progress.
Want to help?
Check out the
jenkinsci/docs gitter channel
.
For other ways to contribute to the Jenkins project, see
this page about participating and contributing
.
Jenkins defines extension points, which are interfaces or abstract classes that model an aspect of its behavior.
The interface or class defines contracts of what needs implementation, and Jenkins allows plugins to contribute those implementations.

input: What changed for the agent-to-controller security system in Jenkins 2.326?
expected_output: Per JEP-235, Jenkins 2.326 removes the ability to disable or customize the agent-to-controller security system, and almost all related configuration code is gone. The notable exception is `jenkins.security.s2m.AdminWhitelistRule`, kept as a stub for Configuration as Code Plugin and Groovy init scripts (it still exposes `#getMasterKillSwitch` and `#setMasterKillSwitch(boolean)`). Several plugins needed updates to remain compatible.
context:
Agent → Controller Security Changes in 2.326
Jenkins 2.326 removes the ability to disable or customize the agent-to-controller security system.
This change is specified in
JEP-235
.
Plugin Compatibility
Several plugins are known to be affected by this change.
For the following plugins, updates are available as of December 29, 2021:
Cobertura Plugin Plugin
needs to be updated to 1.17 or newer.

Their compatibility fixes are tracked in the Jira epic
JENKINS-67173
.
API Compatibility
As part of the change in 2.326, almost all code related to the agent-to-controller security system’s configuration has been removed.
A notable exception is
jenkins.security.s2m.AdminWhitelistRule
as that is used by
Configuration as Code Plugin
and is also expected to be in frequent use in
Groovy init scripts
.
It is now a stub retaining the basic API —
#getMasterKillSwitch
and
#setMasterKillSwitch(boolean)

input: What is the difference between a Multibranch Pipeline and an Organization Folder in Jenkins?
expected_output: A Multibranch Pipeline implements a different Jenkinsfile for each branch of a single project. An Organization Folder goes one level up and monitors an entire GitHub Organization, Bitbucket Team/Project, GitLab organization, or Gitea organization, automatically creating new Multibranch Pipelines for every repository that contains a Jenkinsfile.
context:
use those plugins.
Using Organization Folders
Organization Folders enable Jenkins to monitor an entire GitHub
Organization, Bitbucket Team/Project, GitLab organization, or Gitea organization and automatically create new
Multibranch Pipelines for repositories which contain branches and pull requests
containing a
Jenkinsfile
.
Organization folders are implemented for:
GitHub in the
GitHub Branch Source
plugin
Bitbucket in the
Bitbucket Branch Source
plugin
GitLab in the
GitLab Branch Source

Branches and Pull Requests
In the
previous section
a
Jenkinsfile
which could be
checked into source control was implemented. This section covers the concept of
Multibranch
Pipelines which build on the
Jenkinsfile
foundation to provide
more dynamic and automatic functionality in Jenkins.
Creating a Multibranch Pipeline in Jenkins
Creating a Multibranch Pipeline
The
Multibranch Pipeline
project type enables you to invent different
Jenkinsfiles for different branches of the same project.

input: Where can I find the Javadoc for the Jenkins core APIs my plugin depends on?
expected_output: Jenkins publishes Javadoc for the latest weekly release and for recent LTS releases of core. Stapler, the request routing framework, has its own separate Javadoc as well. Start by choosing the minimum Jenkins core baseline for your plugin, then read the Javadoc matching that version.
context:
Javadoc
Jenkins Core
This is the Javadoc for Jenkins core. Javadoc is available for the latest weekly release and for recent LTS releases.
See choosing a Jenkins baseline for guidance choosing the minimum Jenkins core version for a plugin.

Latest Core Javadoc
LTS
